text: The Emperor lifts his Zhi in silent acknowledgment. Court historians scratch your name into the bamboo slips — not in scandal, but in praise. You leave the hall with the weight of approval and a faint smile from the Grand Chancellor.

Three sips, three rituals, three measured breaths. The court musicians shift to the second melody — a gesture reserved for guests who have proven themselves. By morning, you are remembered as 'the one who knew the rites.'
Your hands do not tremble. Your eyes do not stray. The Son of Heaven himself drinks after you — an honor whispered about in the inner court for decades to come.
The Master of Ceremonies coughs once — a quiet correction, but everyone heard it. You will not be invited to the inner audience, but you keep your post. Better than most.
A junior official catches the slip and substitutes the proper vessel before the Emperor notices. He saves your reputation — and now you owe him a favor that will not be small.
You overfill. A drop of millet wine lands on your sleeve. The court pretends not to see, but at the next banquet, your seat is moved one position further from the throne.

The hall falls into a silence so deep you can hear the lanterns hiss. The Emperor lowers his cup without drinking. By dawn you are escorted from the capital, your family name removed from three registers.
An attendant gently removes the vessel from your hand as if it were a child's toy. You finish the meal in your seat, but no one speaks to you again — not that night, not ever.
The Grand Astrologer notes the breach in his almanac. The next morning, an unfavorable omen is officially attributed to your conduct. You become a minor lesson in three centuries of ritual textbooks.
Your Gu rises in perfect rhythm with the host's. Two governors lean in to ask which province raised you. Trade routes will open.
The host's chief steward raises an eyebrow — the highest praise his face will ever offer. An invitation arrives the next week, sealed in green wax.
You finish your sip the moment the music finishes its phrase. A young noblewoman across the table makes a note. So does her father.
A small misstep — but the host laughs it off. You will be remembered as 'enthusiastic,' which is better than most outcomes here.
Your warming vessel cools too quickly. The wine is served lukewarm. A faint disappointment, but no scandal.
You reach for the wrong cup but recover before pouring. The retainer beside you politely refills the right one. You exhale only after he turns away.
Your delegation will not be invited back. The next caravan from your province is searched at the border. Coincidence, surely.
The host's musicians switch mid-song to a piece traditionally played for the uncultured. Everyone knows. You pretend you don't.
By the time you reach your carriage, three letters criticizing your conduct are already being drafted in three different scriptoria.
Your Jiao rises, full and unapologetic. Friends cheer. Someone declares you the official cup-bearer of every gathering from now until the harvest.
Three sips and one excellent joke later, you are officially the most fun person at the bonfire. The story is retold for years.
You sip with such unstudied grace that an old scholar present mutters, 'The way of the gentleman lives.' He pours you a refill himself.
Wrong cup, but no one really cares. Someone hands you the right one and you keep talking. The lamb is excellent tonight.
You spill a little. The dog appreciates it. So does the host, who has been looking for an excuse to tell his favorite story about the dog.
The one cousin who reads ritual manuals shakes his head. Everyone else shouts him down. You are unanimously declared correct by acclamation.
Even at a backyard gathering, this was bad. People are not angry — they are concerned. Someone offers you water and asks gently if you've eaten today.
Your friends invent a new drinking game in your honor — the goal is to do everything you just did. It is now a regional tradition.
The host laughs so hard he has to sit down. He keeps your wine cup as a souvenir. You will never live this down.
You raise a Bei — a CUP that will not exist for another six hundred years. The court historian drops his brush. The Emperor stares as if you have walked in wearing tomorrow's clothes.
A Han-dynasty cup at a Shang-dynasty banquet. The temporal anomaly is so jarring that two diviners faint. You are politely asked to leave the timeline.
The Master of Rites whispers, 'That… belongs to a future we have not yet built.' You will be written about — but only by historians who specialize in mistakes.
The governors exchange glances. None of them recognize the vessel. You have brought a future to a meeting of the past — and no one will trade with you.
An anachronism this severe is reported to the regional priests. They burn incense for three days to undo whatever cosmic damage you may have caused.
The host's antiquarian asks to inspect your cup. He turns it over twice and pronounces, 'This is not from any period we know.' He is more correct than he realizes.
Your friends raise their bronzes. You raise a tiny later-era cup. They laugh until they cry. One of them takes the cup home as a 'historical curiosity.'
Even at a backyard gathering, the wrongness is undeniable. Someone says, 'Where did you even get that?' You don't have a good answer.
The eldest aunt blesses the cup, just in case it is haunted. The youngest cousin asks if it is from the future. You change the subject.
